title: Towards technology-smart green industrial policy - Nature Energy
gist: A Nature Energy comment argues governments designing green industrial policy should evaluate technology characteristics to strategically pick winners that deliver both global cost-competitiveness and domestic economic payoffs, rather than betting blindly.

insights.claim: Governments can strategically pick winning energy technologies by evaluating technology characteristics, targeting ones that deliver both global cost-competitiveness and domestic economic payoffs.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Reframes industrial-policy risk as a screenable technology-attribute problem, useful for anticipating which clean-tech sectors receive state backing.
insights.quote: policymakers can strategically pick winning technologies that deliver both global cost-competitiveness and domestic economic payoffs

insights.claim: Identifying future winning energy technologies is characterized as increasingly important as governments design green industrial policy, implying rising state intervention in technology selection.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Rising government technology-picking means policy tailwinds will increasingly concentrate on specific favored technologies, not clean-tech broadly.
insights.quote: Identifying future winning energy technologies is increasingly important as governments design green industrial policy

insights.claim: The comment proposes a framework to locate winners and losers among energy technologies, distinguishing global cost-competitiveness from domestic economic payoffs as two separate axes policymakers must weigh.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: A two-axis winner/loser map is a screen investors can apply to guess which technologies survive policy scrutiny in a given country.
insights.quote: Framework to locate winners and losers.

insights.claim: The framing implies a technology can be globally cost-competitive yet still fail domestic-payoff tests, or vice versa, so subsidies should not be allocated on cost curves alone.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Watch for policy that backs domestically-jobs-rich technologies even where they are not the cheapest, distorting pure cost-curve bets.
